question:
Determine, with proof, the set of real numbers $x$ for which
\[
\sum_{n=1}^\infty \left( \frac{1}{n} \csc \frac{1}{n} - 1 \right)^x
\]
converges.
solution:
Solution. Define
\[
a_{n}=\frac{1}{n} \csc \frac{1}{n}-1=\frac{1}{n \sin \frac{1}{n}}-1 .
\]

Taking \( t=1 / n \) in the inequality \( 0<\sin t<t \) for \( t \in(0, \pi) \), we obtain \( a_{n}>0 \), so each term \( a_{n}^{x} \) of the series is defined for any real \( x \). Using \( \sin t=t-t^{3} / 3!+O\left(t^{5}\right) \) as \( t \rightarrow 0 \), we have, as \( n \rightarrow \infty \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
a_{n} & =\frac{1}{n\left(\frac{1}{n}-\frac{1}{6 n^{3}}+O\left(\frac{1}{n^{5}}\right)\right)}-1 \\
& =\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{6 n^{2}}+O\left(\frac{1}{n^{4}}\right)}-1 \\
& =\frac{1}{6 n^{2}}+O\left(\frac{1}{n^{4}}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

In particular, if \( b_{n}=1 / n^{2} \), then \( a_{n}^{x} / b_{n}^{x} \) has a finite limit as \( n \rightarrow \infty \), so by the Limit Comparison Test [Spv, Ch. 22, Theorem 2], \( \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_{n}^{x} \) converges if and only if \( \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_{n}^{x}=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^{-2 x} \) converges, which by the Integral Comparison Test [Spv, Ch. 22, Theorem 4] holds if and only if \( 2 x>1 \), i.e., \( x>1 / 2 \).

Remark (big- \( O \) and little-o notation). Recall that \( O(g(n)) \) is a stand-in for a function \( f(n) \) for which there exists a constant \( C \) such that \( |f(n)| \leq C|g(n)| \) for all sufficiently large \( n \). (This does not necessarily imply that \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} f(n) / g(n) \) exists.)
Similarly " \( f(t)=O(g(t)) \) as \( t \rightarrow 0 \) " means that there exists a constant \( C \) such that \( |f(t)| \leq C|g(t)| \) for sufficiently small nonzero \( t \).

On the other hand, \( o(g(n)) \) is a stand-in for a function \( f(n) \) such that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)}=0
\]

One can similarly define " \( f(t)=o(g(t)) \) as \( t \rightarrow 0 " \).

descriptive_long.question:
Determine, with proof, the set of real numbers exponent for which
\[
\sum_{indexvar=1}^\infty \left( \frac{1}{indexvar} \csc \frac{1}{indexvar} - 1 \right)^{exponent}
\]
converges.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Define
\[
seriesterm=\frac{1}{indexvar} \csc \frac{1}{indexvar}-1=\frac{1}{indexvar \sin \frac{1}{indexvar}}-1 .
\]

Taking \( smallvar=1 / indexvar \) in the inequality \( 0<\sin smallvar<smallvar \) for \( smallvar \in(0, \pi) \), we obtain \( seriesterm>0 \), so each term \( seriesterm^{exponent} \) of the series is defined for any real exponent. Using \( \sin smallvar=smallvar-smallvar^{3} / 3!+O\left(smallvar^{5}\right) \) as \( smallvar \rightarrow 0 \), we have, as \( indexvar \rightarrow \infty \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
seriesterm & =\frac{1}{indexvar\left(\frac{1}{indexvar}-\frac{1}{6 indexvar^{2}}+O\left(\frac{1}{indexvar^{4}}\right)\right)}-1 \\
& =\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{6 indexvar^{2}}+O\left(\frac{1}{indexvar^{4}}\right)}-1 \\
& =\frac{1}{6 indexvar^{2}}+O\left(\frac{1}{indexvar^{4}}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

In particular, if \( compterm=1 / indexvar^{2} \), then \( seriesterm^{exponent} / compterm^{exponent} \) has a finite limit as \( indexvar \rightarrow \infty \), so by the Limit Comparison Test [Spv, Ch. 22, Theorem 2], \( \sum_{indexvar=1}^{\infty} seriesterm^{exponent} \) converges if and only if \( \sum_{indexvar=1}^{\infty} compterm^{exponent}=\sum_{indexvar=1}^{\infty} indexvar^{-2 exponent} \) converges, which by the Integral Comparison Test [Spv, Ch. 22, Theorem 4] holds if and only if \( 2 exponent>1 \), i.e., \( exponent>1 / 2 \).

Remark (big- \( O \) and little-o notation). Recall that \( O(g(indexvar)) \) is a stand-in for a function \( f(indexvar) \) for which there exists a boundconstant such that \( |f(indexvar)| \leq boundconstant|g(indexvar)| \) for all sufficiently large indexvar. (This does not necessarily imply that \( \lim _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty} f(indexvar) / g(indexvar) \) exists.)
Similarly " \( f(smallvar)=O(g(smallvar)) \) as \( smallvar \rightarrow 0 \) " means that there exists a boundconstant such that \( |f(smallvar)| \leq boundconstant|g(smallvar)| \) for sufficiently small nonzero smallvar.

On the other hand, \( o(g(indexvar)) \) is a stand-in for a function \( f(indexvar) \) such that
\[
\lim _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f(indexvar)}{g(indexvar)}=0
\]

One can similarly define " \( f(smallvar)=o(g(smallvar)) \) as \( smallvar \rightarrow 0 " .

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Determine, with proof, the set of real numbers $riverbank$ for which
\[
\sum_{honeycomb=1}^\infty \left( \frac{1}{honeycomb} \csc \frac{1}{honeycomb} - 1 \right)^{riverbank}
\]
converges.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Define
\[
moonlight=\frac{1}{honeycomb} \csc \frac{1}{honeycomb}-1=\frac{1}{honeycomb \sin \frac{1}{honeycomb}}-1 .
\]

Taking \( blueberry=1 / honeycomb \) in the inequality \( 0<\sin blueberry<blueberry \) for \( blueberry \in(0, \pi) \), we obtain \( moonlight>0 \), so each term \( moonlight^{riverbank} \) of the series is defined for any real \( riverbank \). Using \( \sin blueberry=blueberry-blueberry^{3} / 3!+O\left(blueberry^{5}\right) \) as \( blueberry \rightarrow 0 \), we have, as \( honeycomb \rightarrow \infty \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
moonlight & =\frac{1}{honeycomb\left(\frac{1}{honeycomb}-\frac{1}{6 honeycomb^{3}}+O\left(\frac{1}{honeycomb^{5}}\right)\right)}-1 \\
& =\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{6 honeycomb^{2}}+O\left(\frac{1}{honeycomb^{4}}\right)}-1 \\
& =\frac{1}{6 honeycomb^{2}}+O\left(\frac{1}{honeycomb^{4}}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

In particular, if \( sunflower=1 / honeycomb^{2} \), then \( moonlight^{riverbank} / sunflower^{riverbank} \) has a finite limit as \( honeycomb \rightarrow \infty \), so by the Limit Comparison Test [Spv, Ch. 22, Theorem 2], \( \sum_{honeycomb=1}^{\infty} moonlight^{riverbank} \) converges if and only if \( \sum_{honeycomb=1}^{\infty} sunflower^{riverbank}=\sum_{honeycomb=1}^{\infty} honeycomb^{-2 riverbank} \) converges, which by the Integral Comparison Test [Spv, Ch. 22, Theorem 4] holds if and only if \( 2 riverbank>1 \), i.e., \( riverbank>1 / 2 \).

Remark (big- \( O \) and little-o notation). Recall that \( O(g(honeycomb)) \) is a stand-in for a function \( f(honeycomb) \) for which there exists a constant \( raincloud \) such that \( |f(honeycomb)| \leq raincloud|g(honeycomb)| \) for all sufficiently large \( honeycomb \). (This does not necessarily imply that \( \lim _{honeycomb \rightarrow \infty} f(honeycomb) / g(honeycomb) \) exists.)
Similarly " \( f(blueberry)=O(g(blueberry)) \) as \( blueberry \rightarrow 0 \) " means that there exists a constant \( raincloud \) such that \( |f(blueberry)| \leq raincloud|g(blueberry)| \) for sufficiently small nonzero \( blueberry \).

On the other hand, \( o(g(honeycomb)) \) is a stand-in for a function \( f(honeycomb) \) such that
\[
\lim _{honeycomb \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f(honeycomb)}{g(honeycomb)}=0
\]

One can similarly define " \( f(blueberry)=o(g(blueberry)) \) as \( blueberry \rightarrow 0 " .

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Determine, with proof, the set of real numbers $knownvalue$ for which
\[
\sum_{boundless=1}^\infty \left( \frac{1}{boundless} \csc \frac{1}{boundless} - 1 \right)^{knownvalue}
\]
converges.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Define
\[
steadystate=\frac{1}{boundless} \csc \frac{1}{boundless}-1=\frac{1}{boundless \sin \frac{1}{boundless}}-1 .
\]

Taking \( timeless=1 / boundless \) in the inequality \( 0<\sin timeless<timeless \) for \( timeless \in(0, \pi) \), we obtain \( steadystate>0 \), so each term \( steadystate^{knownvalue} \) of the series is defined for any real \( knownvalue \). Using \( \sin timeless=timeless-timeless^{3} / 3!+O\left(timeless^{5}\right) \) as \( timeless \rightarrow 0 \), we have, as \( boundless \rightarrow \infty \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
steadystate & =\frac{1}{boundless\left(\frac{1}{boundless}-\frac{1}{6 boundless^{3}}+O\left(\frac{1}{boundless^{5}}\right)\right)}-1 \\
& =\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{6 boundless^{2}}+O\left(\frac{1}{boundless^{4}}\right)}-1 \\
& =\frac{1}{6 boundless^{2}}+O\left(\frac{1}{boundless^{4}}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

In particular, if \( disorderly=1 / boundless^{2} \), then \( steadystate^{knownvalue} / disorderly^{knownvalue} \) has a finite limit as \( boundless \rightarrow \infty \), so by the Limit Comparison Test [Spv, Ch. 22, Theorem 2], \( \sum_{boundless=1}^{\infty} steadystate^{knownvalue} \) converges if and only if \( \sum_{boundless=1}^{\infty} disorderly^{knownvalue}=\sum_{boundless=1}^{\infty} boundless^{-2 knownvalue} \) converges, which by the Integral Comparison Test [Spv, Ch. 22, Theorem 4] holds if and only if \( 2 knownvalue>1 \), i.e., \( knownvalue>1 / 2 \).

Remark (big- \( O \) and little-o notation). Recall that \( O(g(boundless)) \) is a stand-in for a function \( f(boundless) \) for which there exists a constant \( variable \) such that \( |f(boundless)| \leq variable|g(boundless)| \) for all sufficiently large \( boundless \). (This does not necessarily imply that \( \lim _{boundless \rightarrow \infty} f(boundless) / g(boundless) \) exists.)
Similarly " \( f(timeless)=O(g(timeless)) \) as \( timeless \rightarrow 0 \) " means that there exists a constant \( variable \) such that \( |f(timeless)| \leq variable|g(timeless)| \) for sufficiently small nonzero \( timeless \).

On the other hand, \( o(g(boundless)) \) is a stand-in for a function \( f(boundless) \) such that
\[
\lim _{boundless \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f(boundless)}{g(boundless)}=0
\]

One can similarly define " \( f(timeless)=o(g(timeless)) \) as \( timeless \rightarrow 0 " .

garbled_string.question:
Determine, with proof, the set of real numbers qzxwvtnp for which
\[
\sum_{hjgrksla=1}^\infty \left( \frac{1}{hjgrksla} \csc \frac{1}{hjgrksla} - 1 \right)^{qzxwvtnp}
\]
converges.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Define
\[
fdertyui=\frac{1}{hjgrksla} \csc \frac{1}{hjgrksla}-1=\frac{1}{hjgrksla \sin \frac{1}{hjgrksla}}-1 .
\]

Taking \( plmoknij=1 / hjgrksla \) in the inequality \( 0<\sin plmoknij<plmoknij \) for \( plmoknij \in(0, \pi) \), we obtain \( fdertyui>0 \), so each term \( fdertyui^{qzxwvtnp} \) of the series is defined for any real \( qzxwvtnp \). Using \( \sin plmoknij=plmoknij-plmoknij^{3} / 3!+O\left(plmoknij^{5}\right) \) as \( plmoknij \rightarrow 0 \), we have, as \( hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
fdertyui & =\frac{1}{hjgrksla\left(\frac{1}{hjgrksla}-\frac{1}{6 hjgrksla^{3}}+O\left(\frac{1}{hjgrksla^{5}}\right)\right)}-1 \\
& =\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{6 hjgrksla^{2}}+O\left(\frac{1}{hjgrksla^{4}}\right)}-1 \\
& =\frac{1}{6 hjgrksla^{2}}+O\left(\frac{1}{hjgrksla^{4}}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

In particular, if \( vbnmlkjh=1 / hjgrksla^{2} \), then \( fdertyui^{qzxwvtnp} / vbnmlkjh^{qzxwvtnp} \) has a finite limit as \( hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty \), so by the Limit Comparison Test [Spv, Ch. 22, Theorem 2], \( \sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{\infty} fdertyui^{qzxwvtnp} \) converges if and only if \( \sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{\infty} vbnmlkjh^{qzxwvtnp}=\sum_{hjgrksla=1}^{\infty} hjgrksla^{-2 qzxwvtnp} \) converges, which by the Integral Comparison Test [Spv, Ch. 22, Theorem 4] holds if and only if \( 2 qzxwvtnp>1 \), i.e., \( qzxwvtnp>1 / 2 \).

Remark (big- \( O \) and little-o notation). Recall that \( O(g(hjgrksla)) \) is a stand-in for a function \( f(hjgrksla) \) for which there exists a constant \( asdfghjk \) such that \( |f(hjgrksla)| \leq asdfghjk|g(hjgrksla)| \) for all sufficiently large \( hjgrksla \). (This does not necessarily imply that \( \lim _{hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty} f(hjgrksla) / g(hjgrksla) \) exists.)
Similarly " \( f(plmoknij)=O(g(plmoknij)) \) as \( plmoknij \rightarrow 0 \) " means that there exists a constant \( asdfghjk \) such that \( |f(plmoknij)| \leq asdfghjk|g(plmoknij)| \) for sufficiently small nonzero \( plmoknij \).

On the other hand, \( o(g(hjgrksla)) \) is a stand-in for a function \( f(hjgrksla) \) such that
\[
\lim _{hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f(hjgrksla)}{g(hjgrksla)}=0
\]

One can similarly define " \( f(plmoknij)=o(g(plmoknij)) \) as \( plmoknij \rightarrow 0 " .

kernel_variant.question:
Determine, with proof, the complete set of real triples 
\[
(x,y,z)\in\mathbb R^{3}
\]
for which the alternating series  
\[
\boxed{\,S(x,y,z)\;=\;
\sum_{n=3}^{\infty}(-1)^{n}\,
\frac{\Bigl[
\,3\sec\!\bigl(\tfrac{3}{n^{5/2}}\bigr)-3
\;+\;
2\bigl(\csc\!\bigl(\tfrac{2}{n^{3}}\bigr)-\tfrac{n^{3}}{2}\bigr)
\Bigr]^{\,x}}
{\,n^{\,y}\,(\log n)^{\,z}}}\qquad(\log=\text{natural logarithm})
\]

(a) converges absolutely;  
(b) converges conditionally, i.e. converges but not absolutely. 

(Real powers are understood through the principal branch; the bracket is strictly positive for every \(n\ge 3\), so all terms are real for every real \(x\).)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  Put  
\[
a_{n}:=
3\sec\!\left(\frac{3}{n^{5/2}}\right)-3+
2\left(\csc\!\left(\frac{2}{n^{3}}\right)-\frac{n^{3}}{2}\right),
\qquad n\ge 3 ,
\]
\[
b_{n}:=\frac{a_{n}^{\,x}}{n^{y}(\log n)^{z}}, 
\qquad 
S(x,y,z)=\sum_{n=3}^{\infty}(-1)^{n} b_{n},
\]
and  
\[
p:=3x+y\quad(\text{the effective power of }n).
\]

Step 1.  Precise asymptotics of \(a_{n}\).

Using the standard small-angle expansions  
\[
\sec t=1+\frac{t^{2}}{2}+O(t^{4}),\qquad 
\csc t=t^{-1}+\frac{t}{6}+O(t^{3})\quad(t\to0),
\]
one finds  
\[
\begin{aligned}
3\sec\!\bigl(\tfrac{3}{n^{5/2}}\bigr)-3
   &=\frac{27}{2n^{5}}+O(n^{-10}),\\
2\Bigl(\csc\!\bigl(\tfrac{2}{n^{3}}\bigr)-\tfrac{n^{3}}{2}\Bigr)
   &=\frac{2}{3n^{3}}+O(n^{-9}),
\end{aligned}
\]
hence  
\[
a_{n}= \frac{2}{3n^{3}}+\frac{27}{2n^{5}}+O(n^{-7})
     =\kappa n^{-3}\!\Bigl(1+\rho n^{-2}+O(n^{-4})\Bigr),
\qquad 
\kappa:=\frac{2}{3},\;\; \rho:=\frac{81}{4}.
\]
In particular \(a_{n}>0\) for every \(n\ge3\), so \(a_{n}^{x}\) is well defined for all real \(x\).

Step 2.  Leading form of \(b_{n}\).

Because \(a_{n}\sim\kappa n^{-3}\) with \(\kappa>0\),
\[
a_{n}^{\,x}= \kappa^{\,x}\,n^{-3x}\bigl(1+O(n^{-2})\bigr),
\qquad
|b_{n}|=\frac{\kappa^{\,x}\,(1+O(n^{-2}))}{n^{p}(\log n)^{z}}
         =\bigl(1+O(n^{-2})\bigr)\,f(n),
\]
where  
\[
f(t):=\frac{\kappa^{\,x}}{t^{p}(\log t)^{z}},\quad t\ge3.
\]

Step 3.  Absolute convergence.

The integral test for the positive series \(\sum n^{-p}(\log n)^{-z}\) gives  

* if \(p>1\) the series converges for every \(z\in\mathbb R\);  

* if \(p=1\) it converges precisely when \(z>1\);  

* if \(p<1\) it diverges for every \(z\).

Because \(|b_{n}|/f(n)\to1\), the same criteria apply to \(\sum |b_{n}|\).

Hence  
ABSOLUTE CONVERGENCE \Leftrightarrow   

 (i) \(p>1\);  or (ii) \(p=1\) and \(z>1\).

Step 4.  Necessary vanishing of the terms.

Since \(|b_{n}|\asymp n^{-p}(\log n)^{-z}\),  
\[
|b_{n}|\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0
\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(p>0\Bigr)\;\text{or}\;\Bigl(p=0\text{ and }z>0\Bigr).
\tag{4.1}
\]
Outside (4.1) the series diverges.

Step 5.  Rigorous monotonicity of \(|b_{n}|\).

For the alternating-series (Leibniz) test we must show that  
\(|b_{n+1}|\le |b_{n}|\) for all large \(n\) under the hypotheses of (4.1).

5.1  Factorisation.  
Write  
\[
|b_{n}| = C_{n}\,f(n),\qquad
C_{n}:=\bigl(1+\rho n^{-2}+O(n^{-4})\bigr)^{x}.
\]
A Taylor expansion gives  
\[
C_{n}=1+\frac{\rho x}{n^{2}}+O(n^{-4}),\qquad 
C_{n+1}-C_{n}=O(n^{-3}). \tag{5.1}
\]

5.2  Discrete derivative of \(f\).  
A direct computation yields  
\[
f(t+1)-f(t)= -t^{-p-1}(\log t)^{-z-1}\bigl[p\log t+z+O(1/t)\bigr]
           =-c_{0}\,t^{-p-1}(\log t)^{-z-1}\bigl(1+o(1)\bigr),
\]
with some constant \(c_{0}>0\) (because \(p\ge0\) and \(p,z\) are fixed).

Thus  
\[
f(n+1)-f(n)= -c_{1}\,n^{-p-1}(\log n)^{-z-1}\bigl(1+o(1)\bigr).
\tag{5.2}
\]

5.3  Comparison of the two contributions.  
For large \(n\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
|b_{n+1}|-|b_{n}|
&=C_{n+1}f(n+1)-C_{n}f(n)  \\
&=C_{n+1}\bigl[f(n+1)-f(n)\bigr]+(C_{n+1}-C_{n})f(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
Using (5.1)-(5.2) and \(f(n)\asymp n^{-p}(\log n)^{-z}\), we get  
\[
\bigl|(C_{n+1}-C_{n})f(n)\bigr|
      =O\!\bigl(n^{-3}\bigr)\cdot n^{-p}(\log n)^{-z}
      =O\!\bigl(n^{-p-3}(\log n)^{-z}\bigr),
\]
whereas  
\[
|f(n+1)-f(n)|
      \asymp n^{-p-1}(\log n)^{-z-1}.
\]
Because \(p\le1\) in the region where absolute convergence fails
(see Step 3), we have \(-p-1> -p-3\); hence  
\[
n^{-p-1}(\log n)^{-z-1}\gg n^{-p-3}(\log n)^{-z}\qquad(n\to\infty).
\]
Therefore the negative term \(C_{n+1}[f(n+1)-f(n)]\) dominates and
\(|b_{n+1}|\le |b_{n}|\) for all sufficiently large \(n\).

Consequently \(|b_{n}|\) is eventually monotone decreasing whenever (4.1) is satisfied.

Step 6.  Conditional convergence.

Assume (4.1).  Then \(|b_{n}|\to0\) and is eventually decreasing, so the alternating-series test applies and \(S(x,y,z)\) converges.

Combining with Step 3, conditional (i.e. non-absolute) convergence occurs precisely when the alternating series converges but the series of absolute values diverges, namely  

(a) \(0<p<1\)  (all \(z\));  

(b) \(p=1\) and \(z\le1\);  

(c) \(p=0\) and \(z>0\).

Step 7.  Final classification.

Recall \(p=3x+y\).

1.  Absolute convergence (part (a)):  
 * \(3x+y>1\) (any \(z\));  
 * \(3x+y=1\) with \(z>1\).

2.  Conditional but not absolute convergence (part (b)):  
 * \(0<3x+y<1\) (any \(z\));  
 * \(3x+y=1\) with \(z\le1\);  
 * \(3x+y=0\) with \(z>0\).

3.  Divergence:  
 * \(3x+y<0\);  
 * \(3x+y=0\) with \(z\le0\).

This completes the proof.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher Dimension:  the parameter space is three-dimensional
\((x,y,z)\) instead of one or two.  
• Multiple Small-Angle Expansions:  both a secant and a cosecant term,
each requiring different series expansions, must be combined while
carefully cancelling the dominant \(n^{3}\) singularity of the cosecant.  
• Mixed Growth Rates:  the bracketed expression behaves like
\(n^{-3}\), demanding a non-trivial asymptotic synthesis before any
comparison test can be applied.  
• Absolute vs Conditional Convergence:  the problem asks for a full
classification of both regimes, forcing the solver to invoke and
coordinate the integral test, limit comparison, Leibniz, and Dirichlet
criteria.  
• Edge-Case Analysis:  delicate logarithmic factors at the critical
border \(3x+y=1\) and \(3x+y=0\) must be handled separately.  

These added layers render the enhanced variant markedly more intricate
than either the original problem or the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Determine, with proof, the complete set of real triples 
\[
(x,y,z)\in\mathbb R^{3}
\]
for which the alternating series  
\[
\boxed{\,S(x,y,z)\;=\;
\sum_{n=3}^{\infty}(-1)^{n}\,
\frac{\Bigl[
\,3\sec\!\bigl(\tfrac{3}{n^{5/2}}\bigr)-3
\;+\;
2\bigl(\csc\!\bigl(\tfrac{2}{n^{3}}\bigr)-\tfrac{n^{3}}{2}\bigr)
\Bigr]^{\,x}}
{\,n^{\,y}\,(\log n)^{\,z}}}\qquad(\log=\text{natural logarithm})
\]

(a) converges absolutely;  
(b) converges conditionally, i.e. converges but not absolutely.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  Put  
\[
a_{n}:=
3\sec\!\left(\frac{3}{n^{5/2}}\right)-3+

\[

\[

\]

Using the standard small-angle expansions  
\[

\csc t=t^{-1}+\frac{t}{6}+O(t^{3})\quad(t\to0),

\[
\begin{aligned}

   &=\frac{2}{3n^{3}}+O(n^{-9}),

\[
a_{n}= \frac{2}{3n^{3}}+\frac{27}{2n^{5}}+O(n^{-7})

\kappa:=\frac{2}{3},\;\; \rho:=\frac{81}{4}.

Because \(a_{n}\sim\kappa n^{-3}\) with \(\kappa>0\),
\[

\]

\[
f(t):=\frac{\kappa^{\,x}}{t^{p}(\log t)^{z}},\quad t\ge3.
\]

Step 3.  Absolute convergence.

The integral test for the positive series \(\sum n^{-p}(\log n)^{-z}\) gives  

* if \(p>1\) the series converges for every \(z\in\mathbb R\);  

* if \(p=1\) it converges precisely when \(z>1\);  

* if \(p<1\) it diverges for every \(z\).

Because \(|b_{n}|/f(n)\to1\), the same criteria apply to \(\sum |b_{n}|\).

Hence  
ABSOLUTE CONVERGENCE \Leftrightarrow   

 (i) \(p>1\);  or (ii) \(p=1\) and \(z>1\).

Step 4.  Necessary vanishing of the terms.

Since \(|b_{n}|\asymp n^{-p}(\log n)^{-z}\),  
\[
|b_{n}|\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0
\Longleftrightarrow
\Bigl(p>0\Bigr)\;\text{or}\;\Bigl(p=0\text{ and }z>0\Bigr).
\tag{4.1}
\]
Outside (4.1) the series diverges.

Step 5.  Rigorous monotonicity of \(|b_{n}|\).

For the alternating-series (Leibniz) test we must show that  
\(|b_{n+1}|\le |b_{n}|\) for all large \(n\) under the hypotheses of (4.1).

5.1  Factorisation.  
Write  
\[
|b_{n}| = C_{n}\,f(n),\qquad
C_{n}:=\bigl(1+\rho n^{-2}+O(n^{-4})\bigr)^{x}.
\]
A Taylor expansion gives  
\[
C_{n}=1+\frac{\rho x}{n^{2}}+O(n^{-4}),\qquad 
C_{n+1}-C_{n}=O(n^{-3}). \tag{5.1}
\]

5.2  Discrete derivative of \(f\).  
A direct computation yields  
\[
f(t+1)-f(t)= -t^{-p-1}(\log t)^{-z-1}\bigl[p\log t+z+O(1/t)\bigr]
           =-c_{0}\,t^{-p-1}(\log t)^{-z-1}\bigl(1+o(1)\bigr),
\]
with some constant \(c_{0}>0\) (because \(p\ge0\) and \(p,z\) are fixed).

Thus  
\[
f(n+1)-f(n)= -c_{1}\,n^{-p-1}(\log n)^{-z-1}\bigl(1+o(1)\bigr).
\tag{5.2}
\]

5.3  Comparison of the two contributions.  
For large \(n\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
|b_{n+1}|-|b_{n}|
&=C_{n+1}f(n+1)-C_{n}f(n)  \\
&=C_{n+1}\bigl[f(n+1)-f(n)\bigr]+(C_{n+1}-C_{n})f(n).
\end{aligned}
\]
Using (5.1)-(5.2) and \(f(n)\asymp n^{-p}(\log n)^{-z}\), we get  
\[
\bigl|(C_{n+1}-C_{n})f(n)\bigr|
      =O\!\bigl(n^{-3}\bigr)\cdot n^{-p}(\log n)^{-z}
      =O\!\bigl(n^{-p-3}(\log n)^{-z}\bigr),
\]
whereas  
\[
|f(n+1)-f(n)|
      \asymp n^{-p-1}(\log n)^{-z-1}.
\]
Because \(p\le1\) in the region where absolute convergence fails
(see Step 3), we have \(-p-1> -p-3\); hence  
\[
n^{-p-1}(\log n)^{-z-1}\gg n^{-p-3}(\log n)^{-z}\qquad(n\to\infty).
\]
Therefore the negative term \(C_{n+1}[f(n+1)-f(n)]\) dominates and
\(|b_{n+1}|\le |b_{n}|\) for all sufficiently large \(n\).

Consequently \(|b_{n}|\) is eventually monotone decreasing whenever (4.1) is satisfied.

Step 6.  Conditional convergence.

Assume (4.1).  Then \(|b_{n}|\to0\) and is eventually decreasing, so the alternating-series test applies and \(S(x,y,z)\) converges.

Combining with Step 3, conditional (i.e. non-absolute) convergence occurs precisely when the alternating series converges but the series of absolute values diverges, namely  

(a) \(0<p<1\)  (all \(z\));  

(b) \(p=1\) and \(z\le1\);  

(c) \(p=0\) and \(z>0\).

Step 7.  Final classification.

Recall \(p=3x+y\).

1.  Absolute convergence (part (a)):  
 * \(3x+y>1\) (any \(z\));  
 * \(3x+y=1\) with \(z>1\).

2.  Conditional but not absolute convergence (part (b)):  
 * \(0<3x+y<1\) (any \(z\));  
 * \(3x+y=1\) with \(z\le1\);  
 * \(3x+y=0\) with \(z>0\).

3.  Divergence:  
 * \(3x+y<0\);  
 * \(3x+y=0\) with \(z\le0\).

This completes the proof.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher Dimension:  the parameter space is three-dimensional
\((x,y,z)\) instead of one or two.  
• Multiple Small-Angle Expansions:  both a secant and a cosecant term,
each requiring different series expansions, must be combined while
carefully cancelling the dominant \(n^{3}\) singularity of the cosecant.  
• Mixed Growth Rates:  the bracketed expression behaves like
\(n^{-3}\), demanding a non-trivial asymptotic synthesis before any
comparison test can be applied.  
• Absolute vs Conditional Convergence:  the problem asks for a full
classification of both regimes, forcing the solver to invoke and
coordinate the integral test, limit comparison, Leibniz, and Dirichlet
criteria.  
• Edge-Case Analysis:  delicate logarithmic factors at the critical
border \(3x+y=1\) and \(3x+y=0\) must be handled separately.  

These added layers render the enhanced variant markedly more intricate
than either the original problem or the current kernel variant.
